title: Counting & Combinatorics
learning_objectives: Apply the fundamental counting principle (multiply independent choices).
Decide whether order matters: permutation vs combination.
Compute permutations P(n,k) and combinations C(n,k) without a calculator.
Handle simple restrictions (no repeats, fixed roles, by-category choices).

pedagogical_model.sequence: I do → we do → you do

opening.builds_on: Multiplication and the idea of independent choices.

items.prompt: How many 2-digit codes use digits 1–4 if repeats are allowed?
How many ways to arrange A, B, C in a row?
Pick 1 of 3 shirts and 1 of 2 pants — how many outfits?
Choosing a 2-person team from a group — does order matter? Why?
items.answer: No — {A,B} is the same team as {B,A}; that's a combination.

retrieval_starter.purpose: Retrieves the multiplication principle, factorials, and the order-vs-no-order distinction that decides permutation versus combination.
retrieval_starter.if_students_struggle: If factorials are shaky, write 4! = 4·3·2·1 explicitly and connect to arranging 4 items.

opening.prior_knowledge_bridge: Everything in counting grows from the multiplication principle you just used for outfits. Q5 splits the world in two: when order matters you keep all arrangements (permutations); when it doesn't, you divide out the repeats (combinations). Today you'll pick the right tool and compute it by hand.
opening.learning_intention: By the end you can count outcomes with the multiplication principle and decide between permutations and combinations, computing each without a calculator.
success_criteria: I can multiply independent choices to count outcomes.
I can tell whether a problem needs a permutation or a combination.
I can evaluate P(n,k) and C(n,k) by canceling factorials.

opening_script.move: Check; sort Q5 into the combination bucket.
Bridge: permutations keep order; combinations divide it out.
I do: choose a committee of 3 from 7.

i_do.stem: A committee of 3 people is chosen from a group of 7. How many different committees are possible?
i_do.explanation: A committee is unordered, so use a combination: C(7,3) = (7·6·5)/(3·2·1) = 210/6 = 35.
think_aloud_steps: Does order matter? No — a committee {A,B,C} is the same in any order. Use combinations.
i_do.key_takeaway: Order matters → permutation; order doesn't → combination. Committees, teams, and groups are combinations.

we_do.stem: In how many ways can 5 people line up in a row?
we_do.explanation: A line is an ordered arrangement of all 5: 5! = 5·4·3·2·1 = 120.

immediate_feedback.if_incorrect: Arranging all n distinct items in order is n!, not a combination.

we_do.stem: From 6 books, in how many ways can you choose 2 to take on a trip?
scaffold_hints: Order doesn't matter for which 2 books → combination.

immediate_feedback.if_incorrect: 30 is the ordered count (P(6,2)); divide by 2! because the 2 books' order doesn't matter.

you_do.stem: A restaurant offers 4 appetizers, 5 main courses, and 3 desserts. How many different 3-course meals (one of each) are possible?
How many distinct arrangements are there of the letters in the word MATH?
you_do.explanation: Four distinct letters arranged in order: 4! = 24.

you_do.stem: A team of 2 is selected from 4 women and 3 men. How many teams have exactly 1 woman and 1 man?
you_do.explanation: Choose 1 of 4 women and 1 of 3 men: 4 × 3 = 12.

you_do.stem: How many 3-digit numbers can be formed from the digits 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 if no digit is repeated?
you_do.explanation: No repeats: 5 choices, then 4, then 3 → 5·4·3 = 60 (this is P(5,3)).

sample_held_out_items.stem: In how many ways can 4 distinct paintings be hung in a row?

mastery_check.definition: A topic is mastered at >=85% correct on new/held-out-style questions across >=2 spaced sessions with delay (PRD Section 9.2). Flashcard recall alone is not mastery; application under mixed, delayed conditions is the bar.
conditions: >=85% correct on new/held-out-style items (not flashcard recall) per session
sustained across >=2 spaced sessions with a delay between them
per-topic memory surfaced only after >=20 graded reviews (PRD give-up rule)
per-topic performance surfaced only after >=8 application attempts (PRD give-up rule)
mastery_check.on_fail: Not yet: route every miss to the required error log (tag why: content gap / reasoning flaw / timing / careless / trap), schedule spaced re-practice via topic-aware scheduling, then re-enter the we-do -> you-do loop before re-testing.

citations.name: Khan Academy — Counting, permutations, and combinations
citations.note: Multiplication principle, P(n,k), C(n,k) with worked examples.

citations.name: GMAC — GMAT Focus Edition (official exam overview)
